title: Why Asking for a Raise Feels Like Asking for Love
body:
Why Asking for a Raise Feels Like Asking for Love

Here's the thing: You probably think asking for a raise is about money. It isn't. Not really. What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? It lays bare all your fears, your insecurities, your childhood wounds, and your desperate need for validation. Asking for a raise, for many of you, feels exactly like standing naked before someone you desperately crave approval from, begging them to see your worth. You're not asking for more dollars. You're asking for love. You're asking to be seen, to be valued, to be told you're enough. And that's why it's terrifying.

You’ve been told to quantify your value, to list your achievements, to build a business case. And sure, there’s a practical side to that. But you know, deep down, that the knot in your stomach isn't about the bullet points on your resume. It's about the fear of rejection. It's about the possibility that someone else will look at you, look at your contributions, and say, "No. You're not worth that much to me." Sound familiar? That sting isn't economic. It's existential. It taps into the deepest parts of you that just want to belong, to be cherished, to be told you matter.

The Echoes of Childhood: Money, Love, and Worth

Think back. How was money talked about in your home? Was it a source of stress? A secret? A reward? A constant struggle? For many of us, money was intertwined with love, even if subtly. Maybe your parents worked tirelessly, sacrificing their presence for financial stability, teaching you that money equals love through absence. Or perhaps it was the opposite - money was scarce, and you learned that love had to compensate for lack. These aren't just quaint memories. They're the blueprints for your adult financial psyche. They dictate how you perceive your own worth in the marketplace.

When you ask for a raise, you’re not just negotiating a salary. You’re re-enacting those early dynamics. You’re asking your boss, who often represents an authority figure, to affirm your value. If you grew up feeling you constantly had to prove yourself to earn love, you’ll likely feel that same pressure in a salary negotiation. If you learned that asking for what you need makes you selfish, you’ll struggle to advocate for yourself financially. It's all connected. Your past isn’t just behind you. It’s living in your present, especially in your bank account and your willingness to expand it.

Vicki Robin, in her important work Your Money or Your Life (paid link), pushes us to examine our "life energy" - how much of our precious time and effort we exchange for money. When you don't ask for a raise you deserve, you're essentially devaluing your own life energy. You're saying your time, your skills, your very being, isn't worth as much as it could be. And that's a real statement about self-worth, not just a financial one. It's not about being greedy. It's about honoring the life you're living and the energy you're expending.

The Neuroscience of Financial Fear

This isn't just touchy-feely stuff. Your brain doesn't differentiate neatly between emotional threat and financial threat. When you anticipate rejection, whether it's from a lover or a boss, your amygdala - the brain's fear center - lights up. You experience the same physiological responses: increased heart rate, shallow breathing, muscle tension. Your body is preparing for a fight, flight, or freeze response. It doesn't care if the threat is a saber-toothed tiger or a "no" from HR. The feeling is primal. This is why asking for a raise can feel so physically debilitating for some. It's not just nerves; it's your ancient survival mechanisms kicking in.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk's The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) illustrates how trauma, even subtle developmental trauma, lodges itself in the body. If you experienced financial insecurity or emotional neglect around money in your formative years, your body remembers. It reacts. The tightening in your chest, the clammy hands, the racing thoughts before a salary negotiation - these aren't just random anxieties. They're echoes of past experiences, stored in your cells, triggered by the present moment. You're not just dealing with the present situation; you're carrying the weight of your entire financial history into that room. Does that land?

The Illusion of Scarcity and the Abundance of Self-Doubt

We live in a culture that constantly whispers messages of scarcity. Not enough time, not enough resources, not enough love, and certainly, not enough money. This narrative feeds into your self-doubt. It tells you that if you ask for more, you're taking from someone else. It convinces you that your worth is a fixed pie, and if you claim a bigger slice, someone else will go hungry. This is a lie. This is a story designed to keep you small, to keep you compliant.

Jiddu Krishnamurti spoke extensively about the conditioning of the mind, how our thoughts and beliefs are shaped by society and our past. Your belief about what you're "worth" financially is deeply conditioned. It's not an objective truth. It's a construct, built from years of messages, experiences, and societal norms. And most of those norms actively discourage you from advocating for yourself, especially if you're not a cisgender white man. You're taught to be grateful for what you have, to not rock the boat, to be "realistic." But what if "realistic" is just another word for "limited by your conditioned beliefs"?

Geneen Roth, who writes beautifully about our relationship with food and money, often points out that our external struggles often mirror our internal ones. If you feel perpetually "not enough" inside, you'll likely see that reflected in your bank account or your willingness to ask for what you deserve. The external number is just a mirror. It's reflecting the internal conversation you're having with yourself about your value. Wealth isn't what you accumulate. It's what you stop fearing. And the biggest fear for many is the fear of being seen as unworthy.

Reclaiming Your Value: A Spiritual Practice

Asking for a raise, then, becomes a spiritual practice. It's an act of self-love. It's a declaration of your inherent worth, independent of external validation. It's about dismantling the old narratives that kept you small and stepping into a new story where you are abundant and deserving. This isn't about arrogance. It's about integrity. It's about aligning your external compensation with your internal sense of value.

George Kinder, the pioneer of the Life Planning movement, encourages people to ask themselves real questions about their lives and money. What would you do if you only had 5-10 years to live? What if you knew you had all the money you needed? These questions reveal what truly matters to you. And often, what truly matters requires financial resources. To deny yourself those resources by not advocating for a fair wage is to deny yourself the life you truly want to live. It's a betrayal of your deepest desires.

You have to start by shifting your internal dialogue. Stop seeing the raise conversation as a battle you might lose, but as an opportunity to assert your truth. It's not about being aggressive; it's about being clear. It's about stating your value with calm conviction. It’s about understanding that your worth isn’t up for debate. It simply is. The negotiation is about aligning external circumstances with that undeniable internal truth. Right?!

Jen Sincero's You Are a Badass at Making Money (paid link) is popular for a reason - she gets straight to the core of this. Money isn't evil. Wanting more of it doesn't make you greedy. It makes you a human being with desires, dreams, and a life to live. It's time to reframe your relationship with money from one of fear and scarcity to one of possibility and deservingness.

Practical Steps to Heal and Ask

This isn't about just "thinking positive." It's about doing the work. The inner work and the outer work. You can't just wish for a raise and expect it to appear. You have to take action, but you have to take action from a place of grounded self-worth, not desperate need.

  1. Journal Your Money Story: Seriously, write it all down. What did you hear about money growing up? What were your parents' financial struggles? What beliefs did you internalize? Unearthing these patterns is the first step to dismantling them.
  2. Identify Your Current Value: Objectively list your accomplishments. Quantify your impact. What problems do you solve? What value do you bring to your organization? This isn't bragging; it's data.
  3. Research Market Rates: Know what someone with your skills and experience is earning in your industry and location. This provides a tangible anchor for your request, moving it beyond mere "desire."
  4. Practice the Conversation: Role-play with a trusted friend. Say the words out loud. Feel the discomfort. Let it pass through you. The more you practice, the less terrifying it becomes. You're building a new neural pathway.
  5. Separate Your Self-Worth from the Outcome: This is crucial. Your value as a human being is not dependent on whether you get the raise or not. If they say no, it's a reflection of their budget, their priorities, or their inability to see your worth. It is not a reflection of *your* worth. Repeat that to yourself until you believe it.
  6. Prepare for the "No": What's your plan B? What will you say if they can't meet your request? Can you negotiate for other benefits? Training? A clear path to a raise in 6 months? Don't be caught off guard.
  7. Sit with the Number: Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. The number you want. The number you deserve. How does it feel to even say it out loud to yourself? Do you flinch? Do you feel guilt? Explore that.

Your relationship with money is a mirror. It shows you where you're holding back, where you're afraid, where you're not fully trusting yourself. Asking for a raise is an opportunity to look directly into that mirror and say, "I see you, and I value you." It's an act of radical self-acceptance. It's not about being greedy; it's about being whole.

FAQ

How do I stop feeling guilty about asking for more money?

Guilt often stems from old programming that links money with selfishness or that there isn't enough to go around. Start by questioning these beliefs. Who told you that? Is it actually true? Remind yourself that you're exchanging your valuable time, skill, and energy for compensation. You're not asking for a handout; you're asking for fair exchange. Journaling about your earliest memories of money and scarcity can help uncover the root of this guilt.

What if my boss says no? Does that mean I'm not valued?

Absolutely not. A "no" can mean many things: budget constraints, company policy, or even your boss's own discomfort with financial conversations. It is rarely, if ever, a direct reflection of your inherent worth or contribution. It's a data point about the situation, not a judgment on you. Your value is intrinsic. If you get a "no," ask for feedback, understand the reasons, and discuss a timeline for re-evaluation or other forms of compensation. Don't personalize it.

Is it really a "spiritual practice" to ask for a raise? That sounds a bit much.

It sounds "a bit much" because we've been conditioned to separate money from spirit. But how you earn, spend, and relate to money directly impacts your peace of mind, your freedom, and your ability to live a life aligned with your values. If you're constantly underpaid, struggling, or feeling resentful, that impacts your spirit. When you consciously advocate for your financial well-being, you're honoring your life force, your time, and your potential. That's deeply spiritual work. It's about aligning your inner truth with your outer reality.

Today, right now, I want you to take one small action. Open a blank document or grab a piece of paper. Write down the number you truly believe you are worth, the salary you deserve. Don't censor it. Don't rationalize it. Just write the number. Let's sit with the discomfort of that number for a moment. Just the act of writing it is a declaration. It's the first step in reclaiming your power. It’s the first whisper of a new story you’re ready to tell yourself about your worth.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Explore why asking for a raise can feel deeply personal. Understand the emotional connections to money and validation that make this conversation challenging.
author: Kalesh
